summary: Charity Majors maps out seven common career inflection points for engineering managers, arguing against rigid career planning and hierarchical ladder-climbing in favor of self-knowledge, preserving optionality, and following intrinsic motivation. She emphasizes that management is a career change (not a promotion), advocates for the engineer-manager pendulum, and urges people to prioritize doing work they genuinely love over chasing titles and status.
key_points: Career planning is overrated — focus on self-knowledge and preserving optionality instead of optimizing for hierarchy
Management is a career change, not a promotion, requiring a full reset to beginner mindset for at least two years
Junior engineers pushed into management too early lose technical credibility and optionality, with women disproportionately affected
There is a chronic shortage of good engineering managers — once you have the skill set, you'll be tapped repeatedly whether you want it or not
Senior leaders who miss engineering should have the courage to go back — the prestige of titles is mostly in your head
money_quotes: Management isn't a promotion, it's a change of career. And if you aren't up for resetting the dial to 'NOOB' for a couple years, with all of the ups and downs, anxieties and discombobulations, then you aren't up for management.
When you're an engineer, it seems like managers hold all the power. Two or three years into a managerial role, you should be thoroughly cured of that illusion.
It takes more effort not to be a manager once you're capable of it.

Nobody wants to report to a manager who doesn't want to be there, or is doing the job begrudgingly. Your complaining is toxic, and makes it impossible for you to coach others to have a healthy relationship with their roles.
The higher you climb the ladder, the further removed you are from the work that brings most people intrinsic joy, that feels real.
key_insight: Instead of planning careers around hierarchical advancement, engineers and managers should cultivate self-knowledge, preserve optionality by rotating between roles, and have the courage to choose work they genuinely love over titles and status.
